narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 39, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you've put together an impressive academic profile for law school admission. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your probability of failing the bar at 52.4%. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass on their first try. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which was the strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction since it signals lower performance relative to peers compared to the average decile of 5.53. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed notably in the same direction, again being well below the typical 5.71 average and reinforcing concerns about early academic standing. On a brighter note, your LSAT score had the second-strongest influence, actively pulling the prediction toward success and highlighting one of your standout strengths. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your decile rankings are in the bottom tier, which weighed more heavily than your above-average test scores and GPA in this case. While these early grades pulled against you, they don't define your potential, especially with your LSAT edge showing real aptitude. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings if possible, or apply to programs with more holistic reviews that value your LSAT and GPA strengths. You might also explore post-baccalaureate options to demonstrate improved performance before reapplying. Keep building on what worked—your test scores—and address those early hurdles head-on for a stronger shot next time.
